title: Arrays & Loops
description: Fixed-size arrays and loops are the backbone of C interview solutions — master declaration, access, iteration, 2D arrays, and sizeof.

sections.title: Fixed arrays and access
sections.explanation: C arrays are fixed-size, stack-allocated blocks of contiguous memory. Declare with `type name[size]` or initialize with `type name[] = {...}`. Access elements with `arr[i]` (0-indexed). Arrays do NOT track their own length — you must manage it yourself.

exercise.prompt: Create an array with values {5, 10, 15, 20, 25}. Print the first and last elements. Then change the middle element to 99 and print it. Finally, compute and print the array length using sizeof.

validation.message: First is 5, last is 25, middle changed to 99, length is 5
Use index 4 for last element, assign 99 to index 2, and sizeof for length

exercise.hint: Last element is nums[4]. Set nums[2] = 99. Length = sizeof(nums) / sizeof(nums[0]).

sections.title: for and while loops
sections.explanation: **for** loops are the standard iteration pattern: `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)`. **while** loops are used when the number of iterations isn't known upfront. Both are identical in power — use whichever reads more clearly for the problem.

exercise.prompt: Given array {2, 4, 6, 8, 10}, use a for loop to compute the sum and print it. Then use a while loop to print all elements separated by spaces.

validation.message: Sum should be 30, then print all elements space-separated
Use a for loop with index i and a while loop with index j

exercise.hint: Access arr[i] inside the for loop. The while condition is j < n.

sections.title: 2D arrays
sections.explanation: 2D arrays model grids and matrices — very common in interview problems (BFS on grid, DP tables). Declare as `int grid[ROWS][COLS]`. Access with `grid[row][col]`. Iterate with nested for loops. You must know the dimensions at compile time or use dynamic allocation.

exercise.prompt: Create a 3x3 identity matrix (1s on diagonal, 0s elsewhere) as a 2D array. Print it row by row with spaces between elements.

validation.message: Print the 3x3 identity matrix row by row
Use grid[r][c] to access elements in the 2D array

exercise.hint: Access each element as grid[r][c] where r is the row and c is the column index.

sections.title: sizeof for array length
sections.explanation: Since C arrays don't store their length, use `sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0])` to compute it. This only works for stack-allocated arrays — it fails for pointer parameters (which decay and lose size info). In functions, always pass the length as a separate parameter.

exercise.prompt: Compute and print the length of three arrays: an int array {1,2,3,4,5}, a double array {1.0, 2.0, 3.0}, and a char array "hello" (remember: string literals include a null terminator). Print each length.

validation.message: int array has 5 elements, double has 3, char "hello" has 6 (including null terminator)
Use sizeof for each array to compute length

exercise.hint: Divide sizeof(arr) by sizeof(arr[0]). "hello" has 6 chars: h, e, l, l, o, and the '\0' terminator.
